expanded_description: Understand how atoms share electrons to form covalent bonds, creating stable molecules with specific structures and properties distinct from ionic compounds.

core_concept.explanation: Covalent bonds form when two nonmetal atoms share one or more pairs of valence electrons to achieve stable electron configurations (typically octets). Unlike ionic bonding where electrons transfer, covalent bonding involves mutual attraction of both nuclei for the shared electron pair(s).

image.alt: Diagram showing two hydrogen atoms sharing electrons to form H₂
image.caption: Figure 6.1.1: Covalent bond formation—two hydrogen atoms share their single valence electrons, creating a stable $\text{H}_2$ molecule with a filled $1s$ orbital for each atom.

sections.introduction: Covalent bonding occurs primarily between nonmetal atoms with similar electronegativities. Instead of transferring electrons (as in ionic bonding), atoms share electrons to fill their valence shells.

key_concept.definition: A covalent bond is a chemical bond formed by the sharing of one or more pairs of electrons between two atoms, resulting in a stable balance of attractive and repulsive forces.
key_concept.context: Covalent bonds are directional and form discrete molecules or extended networks (e.g., diamond, quartz).

characteristics: Shared electrons occupy the overlapping region
Both nuclei attract the shared electron pair
classifications.behavior: As atoms approach, attractive forces (nucleus-electron) initially dominate; at optimal distance (bond length), attraction balances repulsion (nucleus-nucleus, electron-electron).

examples.explanation: Two hydrogen atoms, each with one unpaired electron, share their electrons to form a single covalent bond in $\text{H}_2$.

image.caption: Electron sharing in hydrogen molecule formation.

examples.explanation: Two chlorine atoms share one electron pair to complete their octets, forming $\text{Cl}_2$.

characteristics: Stronger bonds have higher bond energies
classifications.behavior: The bond dissociation energy quantifies the stability: higher energy required to break the bond indicates greater stability.

examples.explanation: Breaking the $\text{H}-\text{H}$ bond requires $436~\text{kJ/mol}$; forming it releases the same amount.

image.alt: Potential energy curve for H₂ bond
image.caption: Energy minimum at bond length represents stable $\text{H}_2$.

principles.statement: Atoms share electrons to achieve eight valence electrons (or two for hydrogen), mimicking noble gas configurations.
principles.application: In $\text{CH}_4$, carbon shares four electron pairs with four hydrogen atoms, giving carbon an octet and each hydrogen a duet.
principles.analogy: Like roommates sharing a apartment: each contributes resources (electrons) to achieve a comfortable living situation (stable configuration) neither could afford alone.

principles.principle: Bond Length and Bond Strength Relationship
principles.statement: Shorter covalent bonds are generally stronger because the shared electrons are held more tightly between closer nuclei.
principles.application: $\text{C}-\text{C}$ single bond ($154~\text{pm}$, $347~\text{kJ/mol}$) is longer and weaker than $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond ($134~\text{pm}$, $614~\text{kJ/mol}$).
principles.example: Triple bonds ($\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$) are shortest and strongest due to three shared pairs pulling nuclei closer.

sections.introduction: The arrangement of atoms in a molecule—its molecular structure—is determined by how covalent bonds form and how electron pairs repel each other (explored further in VSEPR theory, Section 6.5).

key_concept.definition: Molecular structure refers to the three-dimensional arrangement of atoms in a molecule, dictated by the number and type of covalent bonds each atom forms.
key_concept.context: Structure determines properties: shape affects polarity, reactivity, and biological function.

components.definition: Key structural features of covalent molecules
components.interpretation: Total valence electrons determine bonding capacity and lone pair count, which govern molecular geometry.

image.alt: Diagram showing bond angles, lone pairs, and atomic connectivity
image.caption: Figure 6.1.2: Structural elements—bonding pairs (lines), lone pairs (dots), and atomic positions define molecular shape.

categories.description: Valence electrons in molecules are either shared in bonds (bonding pairs) or localized on a single atom (lone pairs).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs, influencing molecular geometry through electron-pair repulsion.

examples.explanation: Oxygen has two bonding pairs (to H) and two lone pairs; lone pairs compress the $\text{H}-\text{O}-\text{H}$ angle to $104.5^\circ$.

image.caption: Lone pairs (red) and bonding pairs (black) in water.

examples.explanation: Nitrogen has three bonding pairs and one lone pair, giving trigonal pyramidal geometry.

image.caption: Lone pair on nitrogen distorts bond angles from ideal tetrahedral.

categories.description: The first bond formed between any two atoms is a sigma bond, created by head-on orbital overlap along the internuclear axis.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Sigma bonds allow free rotation (in single bonds) and provide the primary framework of molecular structure.

examples.explanation: Single bond is a $\sigma$ bond from $sp^3$-$sp^3$ overlap; free rotation enables conformational isomers.

image.caption: Head-on overlap creating a $\sigma$ bond along the bond axis.

principles.statement: Covalent bonds form when atomic orbitals overlap and the overlapping region contains two electrons with opposite spins.
principles.application: In $\text{HF}$, hydrogen's $1s$ orbital overlaps with fluorine's $2p$ orbital, creating a $\sigma$ bond with the shared pair localized between nuclei.
principles.analogy: Like two flashlights shining toward each other: the brightest region (highest electron density) is where the beams overlap.

sections.introduction: Not all covalent bonds share electrons equally. Differences in electronegativity create a spectrum from pure covalent to ionic character.

key_concept.definition: Electronegativity ($\chi$) is an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a covalent bond; differences ($\Delta\chi$) determine bond polarity.
key_concept.context: Bond character is a continuum: pure covalent ($\Delta\chi = 0$) $\rightarrow$ polar covalent ($0 < \Delta\chi < 1.7$) $\rightarrow$ ionic ($\Delta\chi \geq 1.7$).

characteristics: Equal or nearly equal electron sharing
classifications.behavior: Electron density is symmetrically distributed between nuclei; bond dipole moment $\approx 0$.

examples.explanation: Identical atoms share electrons equally; pure nonpolar covalent bond.
Small electronegativity difference; treated as nonpolar in organic chemistry.

classifications.behavior: Electron density shifts toward the more electronegative atom, creating a bond dipole: $\vec{\mu} = q \times d$.

examples.explanation: Fluorine attracts shared electrons strongly; $\text{H}^{\delta+}-\text{F}^{\delta-}$ with significant dipole.

image.caption: Electron density shifted toward fluorine in $\text{H}-\text{F}$.

examples.explanation: Oxygen is more electronegative; $\text{C}^{\delta+}-\text{O}^{\delta-}$ polarity influences reactivity.

principles.statement: Bond dipole moment $\mu$ (in Debye, D) measures polarity: $\mu = q \times d$, where $q$ is partial charge and $d$ is bond length.
principles.application: $\text{H}-\text{F}$ has $\mu = 1.91~\text{D}$; $\text{H}-\text{Cl}$ has $\mu = 1.08~\text{D}$, reflecting greater polarity in $\text{H}-\text{F}$ despite shorter bond length.
principles.analogy: Like a tug-of-war: the stronger team (more electronegative atom) pulls the rope (electron pair) closer, creating an imbalance (dipole).

sections.introduction: Apply covalent bonding principles to predict bond formation, polarity, and molecular stability.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Covalent Bond Formation
worked_examples.problem: Will nitrogen ($\text{N}$) and hydrogen ($\text{H}$) form a covalent bond? If so, how many bonds and what is the molecular formula?

step_by_step.explanation: N (Group 15) has 5 valence $e^-$; H (Group 1) has 1 valence $e^-$. Both are nonmetals.
N needs 3 more $e^-$ for octet; each H needs 1 more $e^-$ for duet.
step_by_step.calculation: N requires 3 bonds; each H forms 1 bond

step_by_step.explanation: One N atom bonds to three H atoms via three single covalent bonds.
Total valence $e^-$: $5 + 3(1) = 8$. In $\text{NH}_3$: 3 bonds (6 $e^-$) + 1 lone pair on N (2 $e^-$) = 8 $e^-$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Yes, nitrogen and hydrogen form covalent bonds. The stable molecule is ammonia, $\text{NH}_3$, with three $\text{N}-\text{H}$ single bonds and one lone pair on nitrogen.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Octet Rule and Covalent Bond Formation

worked_examples.title: Assessing Bond Polarity
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following bonds in order of increasing polarity: $\text{C}-\text{H}$, $\text{O}-\text{H}$, $\text{N}-\text{H}$, $\text{F}-\text{H}$. Use electronegativity values: $\chi_\text{H}=2.1$, $\chi_\text{C}=2.5$, $\chi_\text{N}=3.0$, $\chi_\text{O}=3.5$, $\chi_\text{F}=4.0$.

step_by_step.explanation: Larger $\Delta\chi$ corresponds to greater bond polarity.
$\text{C}-\text{H}$ is nearly nonpolar; $\text{F}-\text{H}$ is highly polar (approaching ionic).

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing polarity: $\text{C}-\text{H} < \text{N}-\text{H} < \text{O}-\text{H} < \text{F}-\text{H}$. The $\text{F}-\text{H}$ bond is the most polar due to fluorine's highest electronegativity.

worked_examples.title: Bond Energy and Stability Prediction
worked_examples.problem: Given bond energies: $\text{C}-\text{C} = 347~\text{kJ/mol}$, $\text{C}=\text{C} = 614~\text{kJ/mol}$, $\text{C}\equiv\text{C} = 839~\text{kJ/mol}$. Which carbon-carbon bond is strongest? Why does bond strength increase with bond order?

step_by_step.explanation: Higher bond energy = stronger bond = more stable.
Bond order = number of shared electron pairs: single=1, double=2, triple=3.
step_by_step.calculation: Triple bond has 3 shared pairs vs. 1 for single bond

step_by_step.explanation: More shared pairs increase electron density between nuclei, enhancing attractive forces and shortening bond length.
step_by_step.calculation: Higher electron density $\rightarrow$ stronger nucleus-electron attraction $\rightarrow$ higher bond energy

worked_examples.final_answer: The $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bond is strongest ($839~\text{kJ/mol}$). Bond strength increases with bond order because additional shared electron pairs increase the attractive force between nuclei and the bonding electrons, pulling atoms closer and requiring more energy to separate them.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bond Order, Length, and Energy Relationships

summary: Covalent bonds form when nonmetal atoms share valence electrons to achieve stable electron configurations. Bond formation releases energy, creating stable molecules with specific structures. The octet rule guides bonding patterns, while electronegativity differences determine bond polarity on a continuum from nonpolar covalent to ionic. Molecular structure arises from the arrangement of bonding and nonbonding electron pairs, with sigma bonds providing the primary framework. Bond strength increases with bond order (single < double < triple) due to greater electron density between nuclei.
